coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots for developing highly flame retardant poly (vinyl alcohol) composite films
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Shu-juan
Lu, Shi-yan
Tan, Deng-feng
Zhu, Yong-fei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,P-CDs) were synthesized from m-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid in a one-step hydrothermal method. Then, novel flame retardant PVA composite films (PVA/N,P-CDs) were prepared by introducing N,P-CDs into the PVA matrix via solvent mixing. Subsequently, the flame retardancy, thermal stability, mechanical properties and transparency of the PVA/N,P-CDs films were investigated through vertical burning tests (UL-94), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), mechanical property tests and UV–visible spectroscopy, respectively. In addition, the structure of the residual char of the composite films was studied by SEM, EDS, in situ FTIR and Raman spectroscopy, and the corresponding flame retardant mechanisms were analysed. It was demonstrated that the addition of a small amount of N,P-CDs endows films with remarkable anti-dripping performance. The limiting oxygen index (LOI) of the PVA/N,P-CDs composite films increased to more than 30% and achieved a UL94 VTM-0 rating when the N,P-CDs solution (the N,P-CDs are diluted 30 times) was higher than 20%. Moreover, the introduction of N,P-CDs solution increased the residual char from 12.4% for neat PVA to 36.4% for PVA/N,P-CDs. These improvements are attributed to the N,P-CDs in the N,P-CDs solution, which are contribute to the formation of a larger char barrier layers, and a less phosphoric acid in the N,P-CDs solution can effectively promote the dehydration, crosslinking and cyclization of PVA matrix to form a close and continuous char barrier layers. Importantly, N,P-CDs considerably improve the high temperature thermostability and mechanical properties of PVA films without affecting transparency and endow these films with UV absorption ability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
N,P co-doped
Flame retardant PVA film
Flame retardant mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of N,P-CDs
Characterization and transparency of PVA/N,P-CDs composite films
Mechanical properties of PVA/N,P-CDs composite films
Thermal stability and fire behavior of PVA/N,P-CDs composite films
Analysis of the residual char and flame retardant mechanisms

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Innovation and entrepreneurship training program for Guangxi District
Guangxi Natural Science Foundation Project of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation Project of China (No. 2020GXNSFBA159038) and the Innovation and entrepreneurship training program for Guangxi District college students in 2020.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images, (b) particle size distribution curve, (c) HR-TEM images, and (d) XRD pattern of N,P-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of N,P-CDs. The (b) XPS, (c) C1s, (d) O1s, (e) N1s, and (f) P2p spectra of N,P-CDs.

(a) FTIR spectra of PVA, N,P-CDs and PVA/N,P-CDs30 composite films; (b) UV–vis transmittance spectra of PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs composite films; Digital Photographs of pure PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs composite films under (c) sunlight and (d) 365 nm UV lamp; AFM image of (e) PVA, (f) PVA/N,P-CDs20 and (g) PVA/N,P-CDs30.

Tensile properties of pure PVA and PVA/N,P-CD films, (a) typical stress − strain curves, (b) tensile strength, (c) elongation at break, and (d) schematic of hydrogen bonds formed by interactions between N,P-CDs and PVA chains. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) TGA weight loss and corresponding (b) DTG curves of pure PVA, PVA/N,P-CDs10, PVA/N,P-CDs20 and PVA/N,P-CDs30 films at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen.
Photos of UL 94 test of pure PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs composite films.
HRR curves of pure PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs composite films.
In situ FTIR spectra of (a) pure PVA and (b) PVA/N,P-CDs composite films.

SEM images and EDS spectra of the char residue of PVA/N,P-CDs films. SEM images of (a) and (b) with the magnification of 10 K and 50 K, respectively; The EDS diagrams and elements contents are shown in (c); The distribution of C, N, O and P at 12.5 K magnification is shown in (d), (e), (f) and (g), respectively.
Raman spectra of the char residues of (a) pure PVA and (b) PVA/N,P-CDs film.
Preparation process of PVA/N,P-CDs composite films.
Possible mechanisms of N,P-CDs flame retardant PVA films.
TGA data of PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs composite films.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: T10 wt%: the temperature of 10% mass loss; T1max and T2max: the first and second peak temperatures in the DTG curves.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: LOI, UL 94 and MCC test data for PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs composite films.

row.entry: After flame and Afterglow time t1/t2 (s)

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots for developing highly flame retardant poly (vinyl alcohol) composite films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,P-CDs) were synthesized from m-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid in a one-step hydrothermal method. Then, novel flame retardant PVA composite films (PVA/N,P-CDs) were prepared by introducing N,P-CDs into the PVA matrix via solvent mixing. Subsequently, the flame retardancy, thermal stability, mechanical properties and transparency of the PVA/N,P-CDs films were investigated through vertical burning tests (UL-94), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), mechanical property tests and UV–visible spectroscopy, respectively. In addition, the structure of the residual char of the composite films was studied by SEM, EDS, in situ FTIR and Raman spectroscopy, and the corresponding flame retardant mechanisms were analysed. It was demonstrated that the addition of a small amount of N,P-CDs endows films with remarkable anti-dripping performance. The limiting oxygen index (LOI) of the PVA/N,P-CDs composite films increased to more than 30% and achieved a UL94 VTM-0 rating when the N,P-CDs solution (the N,P-CDs are diluted 30 times) was higher than 20%. Moreover, the introduction of N,P-CDs solution increased the residual char from 12.4% for neat PVA to 36.4% for PVA/N,P-CDs. These improvements are attributed to the N,P-CDs in the N,P-CDs solution, which are contribute to the formation of a larger char barrier layers, and a less phosphoric acid in the N,P-CDs solution can effectively promote the dehydration, crosslinking and cyclization of PVA matrix to form a close and continuous char barrier layers. Importantly, N,P-CDs considerably improve the high temperature thermostability and mechanical properties of PVA films without affecting transparency and endow these films with UV absorption ability.

ce:section.ce:para: Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) is a water-soluble polymer with biocompatibility and biodegradability, It is widely used in traditional fibres, adhesives, packaging materials and biomedical devices [1–4]. Recently, PVA has shown potential in wearable devices and flexible solar cells due to its excellent transparency, film-forming ability, mechanical properties, and high chemical resistance [5,6]. Unfortunately, PVA has poor flame retardancy with a limited oxygen index (LOI) of approximately 19.4%, indicating that it is easily ignitable. Moreover, PVA burns rapidly, the melt-dropping behaviour is substantial, and melt droplets considerably promote flame spread and may cause casualties [7]. These deficiencies have prevented the practical application of PVA materials in some areas due to fire protection requirements [8].

To address PVA flammability, the addition of flame retardants (FRs), such as halides and metal hydroxides, as well as phosphorus (P)-, nitrogen (N)- and silicon (Si)-containing compounds, are the most convenient solutions [9,10]. As halogen based FRs, particularly brominated FRs, were discovered to adversely affect the ecological environment and human health, their use has continuously decreased and has even been forbidden [11,12]. Metal hydroxides, such as aluminium and magnesium hydroxides, reduce the fire risk of polymers by releasing nonflammable water molecules to cool the solid phase and are considered major commercial alternatives to halogenated FRs. However, the low efficiency of metal hydroxides usually requires very high loading (generally ≥ 50 wt%) and normally adversely affects the mechanical and transparency properties of the flame retardant film [13]. Recently, an increasing number of studies have been performed to examine P- and N/Si-containing FRs because they can form a complete and continuous carbon layer in the condensed phase, thus increasing the flame retardancy while reducing toxicity. Some FRs containing P/N and P/Si have been applied to PVA films. For example, Chen et al. [14] synthesized polyelectrolytes from phenyl phosphonic acid and polyethyleneimine and prepared a flame-resistant triboelectric polymer film with a solution blended with PVA. Their results indicated that the film showed excellent heat resistance and flame retardancy. Liu et al. [8] reported a flame retardant PVA film obtained by coating P-containing siloxane compounds on the surface of the PVA film. The flame retardant was attached to the film surface through the reaction with the PVA hydroxyl group. The flame retardancy of the final film was greatly improved while maintaining high transparency. The abovementioned investigations promoted the development of FR-PVA film materials. However, some problems remain to be solved, such as the complicated preparation process, high cost and potential environmental problems of flame retardant films.

In recent years, carbon nanomaterials have been proposed as flame retardant materials; these include multiarmed carbon nanotubes, graphene and graphene oxide (GO). Although graphene/GO and its derivatives have good flame retardancy in a polymer matrix, the cost of graphene is relatively high. Therefore, it is necessary to produce a simple, environmentally friendly, mass-producible and efficient alternative to carbon-based FRs. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), which are nanoscale carbon-based materials, have become a research focus in recent years. They are widely used in ion detection, cell labelling and sensing devices (in addition to other uses) due to their excellent biocompatibility, water solubility, nontoxicity and easily modified surface molecules [15,16]. Moreover, the size distribution of fluorescent CDs is small and narrow (usually < 10 nm). Abundant hydrophilic functional groups appear on the surface, thus forming hydrogen bonds with hydrophilic polymer chains at the molecular level. Furthermore, CDs can give new functional properties to hydrophilic polymer films without affecting the transparency. Most importantly, fluorescent CDs can be doped with N, P and other heteroatoms and display their potential flame retardant applications. Until recently, there have been only a few reports on the addition of flame-retardant fluorescent CDs to polymer materials. The Rahimi-Aghdam group [17,18] synthesized N-doped CDs, N,P co-doped graphene quantum dots (NPGQDs) and NPGCD-ZnAl-layered double hydroxide complexes, with citric acid as the main raw material, and they were applied to improve the flame retardancy of polyacrylonitrile (PAN). The results suggested that during combustion, the modified PAN composite material formed a better protective char-barrier layer than pure PAN, which significantly improved the flame retardancy of PAN. Unfortunately, the addition of these FRs seriously affects the transparency of PAN materials, creating a dark colour and reducing the transparency.

In this work, N,P-CDs are synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal strategy from m-phenylene-diamine and phosphoric acid. Then, PVA/N,P-CDs composite films is prepared via a simple and environmentally friendly casting film forming process. The flame retardancy, thermal degradation, mechanical properties and transparency of PVA/N,P-CDs composite films were systematically studied. Moreover, the flame retardant mechanism of PVA/N,P-CDs film is explored and explained in detail.

m-Phenylenediamine (MPDA) and phosphoric acid were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, PR China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 1750 ± 50, 99+% hydrolyzed) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, PR China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The N,P-CDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method as described by Yang et al. [19]. Briefly, 0.3 g of MPDA was mixed with 15 mL of phosphoric acid within a Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated at 220 °C for 24 h. After the autoclave naturally cooled to room temperature, the solution was separated at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove impurities. Finally, the supernatant was collected and diluted to 30 times for flame retardancy of PVA film.

The PVA/N,P-CDs composite films were fabricated by a simple solution casting method. The preparation process is shown in Scheme 1 . First, PVA was stirred and dissolved in deionized water at 90 °C to prepare PVA solution with mass concentration of 7.5 wt%. Then, the calculated amount of PVA solution and the 30 times diluted N,P-CDs solution were mixed evenly by mechanical stirring. Subsequently, the mixed solution was cast onto a glass plate and dried at room temperature for 12 h. Finally, the PVA/N,P-CDs composite films were dried at 70 °C for 12 h in an oven to remove residual moisture. The thickness of the flame retardant PVA films were controlled at 0.1 mm. The flame retardant PVA films were named according to the N,P-CDs-diluent weight percentages of 10, 15, 20, 25 and 30 wt%: PVA/N,P-CDs10, PVA/N,P-CDs15, PVA/N,P-CDs20, PVA/N,P-CDs25 and PVA/N,P-CDs30, respectively.

The microstructure of N,P-CDs was observed via transmission electron micros-copy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL Ltd, Japan). The crystalline structure of N,P-CDs was measure by powder X-Ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany), 2θ range is 5-40°. FTIR spectra of N,P-CDs, PVA, and PVA/N,P-CDs films were surveyed on IR spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet iS5, Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd, U.S.A). The elementary composition of N,P-CDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd, U.S.A). The visible light transmittance of films were measured by ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV2600, Shimadzu, Japan), Thermogravimetric analysis was carried out with the Thermogravimetric-mass Spectrometry (TGA, Discovery-Discovery series). Tensile property testing was carried out using universal testing machine (Shimadzu AG5-X LON-10KN, Japan). The limiting oxygen index (LOI) values and vertical combustion (UL-94) experiment were measured on ZR-01 oxygen index meter (Qingdao, China) and CZF-6 horizontal and vertical combustion tester (Nanjing, China), respectively. The film sample of LOI experiment dimensions is 140 × 52 × 0.10 mm3 according to ASTM D2863-17, and experimental data were obtained by testing the average of five films. Vertical combustion experiment dimensions is 200 × 50 × 0.10 mm3 according to ASTM D4804-14 standard. The heat release rate of film was measured on micro calorimeter (MCC, MCC-2, Govmark Ltd, U.S.A) by heating 5–6 mg of the sample at a heating rate of 1 °C/s from room temperature to 740 °C. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Nicolet iN10, Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd, U.S.A) spectra are performed to analysis the composition of the material during the combustion and residual carbon of the films. The microsurface morphology and energy-dispersive spectrometry of the film burned condensed phase were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, QUANTA FEG 400, FEI Company, U.S.A) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS), respectively. Roman spectra were recorded using micro confocal Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR Evolution, HORIBA Scientific, France) with 532 nm excitation. The Micro-surface of PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs30 was surveyed on atomic force microscope (AFM, Multimode V, Veeco Ltd, U.S.A). The dispersion state of the N,P-CDs in PVA film is observed by a polarizing microscope (PLM, 59XC-PC, Shanghai Shangguang Technology Ltd, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of N,P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size characteristics of N,P-CDs were easily observed via TEM. Fig. 1 a shows that N,P-CDs have a primary orbicular shape and uniform distribution. Fig. 1b displays that the size distribution curve of N,P-CDs, and the sizes of N,P-CDs are in the range of 6.0–9.0 nm and Gaussian fitting provides an average size of 7.5 nm. Furthermore, the HR–TEM image in Fig. 1c suggests that the carbon structure is somewhat crystalline and a discernible lattice spacing of approximately 0.26 nm, which demonstrates that N,P-CDs have carbon core that consist of both graphitic and amorphous carbons [20,21]. The XRD pattern of the N,P-CDs in Fig. 1d shows a broad diffraction peak at around 2θ = 23-33°, which is corresponding to the (002) diffraction patterns of the graphitic carbon [22]. Fig. S1 illustrates the UV − vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectrum and digital photographs of the N,P-CDs under sunlight and a 365 nm UV lamp. The N,P-CDs have a strong absorption peak for 350–500 nm, which is attributable to the n − π* transition of the C = O bond and the π − π* aromatic fluorophore structure. [23,24] The illustration in Fig. S1 gives information that the N,P-CDs are a light yellow liquid under sunlight. They emit bright green fluorescence under a 365-nm UV lamp with the maximum excitation wavelength of 442 nm and the emission wavelength of 515 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of the N,P-CDs was measured by the reference method (Fig. S2), the results of which show a high FQY of 67.53%.

The surface functional group and chemical composition of the N,P-CDs were determined by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the FTIR spectrum of N,P-CDs exhibits O–H and N–H stretching vibration bands at 3150–3450 cm−1. The peak at 2921 cm−1 is attributable to the vibrations of C–H, the signal at 1647 cm−1 is attributable to the stretching vibration of the C=C bond, and the weak peak at 1384 cm−1 is attributable to the existence of C–N stretching vibrations. The broad and strong peaks at 1120 cm−1 and 949 cm−1 are attributable to the bending vibration of P=O and P–O–R (R = alkyl group) in the phosphate, respectively [25]. Additionally, the broadband at 544 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of phosphates [19,24]. These results imply that there are a lot of amino and phosphate functional groups on the surface of N,P-CDs.

XPS was adopted to investigate the chemical composition and elemental contents of N,P-CDs. According to the XPS full-scan results presented in Fig. 2b, predominant peaks are at 133.93, 284.77, 399.84 and 531.95 eV, and these peaks are attributable to P2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. This information indicate that the elemental composition of N,P-CDs comprises C, N, O and P, and the content of C, N, O and P accounted for 28.9, 3.61, 55.12 and 12.37%, respectively. Two resolved peaks in the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) are at 284.61 and 285.45 eV, which are attributable to the C–C/C=C and C–N/C–P=O groups, respectively. The majority of the resolved peaks at 534.1, 532.18 and 532.99 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) correspond to the C=O, P–O and C–O–C/O–H groups, respectively. Two highly resolved peaks in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) occur at 399.8 and 401.6 eV, which are attributable to amino-N and pyrrolic-N, respectively [26]. Meanwhile, the 133.73 and 134.43 eV peaks in the P2p spectrum (Fig. 2f) are assigned to the P–O and P–C groups, respectively [16,27]. Therefore, the XPS spectra further confirmed that a lot of amino and phosphate groups exist on the surface of the N,P-CDs, which is consistent with the results of the FTIR analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and transparency of PVA/N,P-CDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of pure PVA, N,P-CDs and PVA/N,P-CDs composite films are exhibited in Fig. 3 a. Compared with pure PVA, the characteristic peak (C–N, P=O and P–OH) of N,P-CDs appears in the PVA/N,P-CDs30 film. The absorption peak of C–N appeared at 1324 cm−1, and the peaks at 1093 and 984 cm−1 were assigned to the P=O and P–OH groups, respectively [25]. Furthermore, the O–H stretching of PVA at 3254 cm−1 decreased and shifted to 3236 cm−1 after the incorporation of N,P-CDs, and the O–H bending of bound water at 1652 cm−1 of PVA similarly shifted to 1647 cm−1 in the PVA/N,P-CDs30 film. In general, the hydroxyl stretching vibration is prone to the association or disassociation of hydrogen bonding, that will decrease the stretching frequency of O-H bonds [28]. Thus, the FTIR results indicated the formation of hydrogen bonding in N,P-CDs and PVA/N,P-CDs composite films.

The good transparency of the film is crucial to its application. We tested the transparency of pure PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs composite films, and the results are shown in Fig. 3b. The pure PVA film had the highest transmittance in the range of 200–700 nm, and the transmittance reached 90% in the visible region. For PVA/N,P-CDs composite films, the light transmittance successively declined with an increase in the N,P-CDs content, particularly in the UV spectrum region. This phenomenon is attributable to the UV absorption characteristics of the N,P-CDs. Subsequently, the PVA/N,P-CDs composite film transmittance remained constant in the visible region because N,P-CDs rarely absorb light in this region. As shown in the digital photograph of PVA/N,P-CDs composite films under sunlight (Fig. 3c), the films are highly transparent under visible light. Their transparency is not affected by the increase in the concentration of the N,P-CDs solution up to 30 wt%. The school badge under the film is still visible.

Generally speaking, the uniformity of flame retardant dispersion in polymer matrix will affect the flame retardancy of materials [29]. Therefore, we observed the fluorescence emission of N,P-CDs in the film through a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp to investigate the dispersion properties of carbon dots in the PVA film. The results are shown in Fig. 3d. Pure PVA film had no fluorescence response. In PVA/N,P-CDs composite films, the fluorescence intensity enhanced when the concentration of N,P-CDs solution was increased from 10% to 30%. When the concentration is higher than 20%, the fluorescence enhancement becomes not obvious.

The AFM images (Fig. 3e, f and g) of the PVA, PVA/N,P-CDs20 and PVA/N,P-CDs30 films show that N,P-CDs are evenly distributed in PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs20 films without agglomeration. However, the dark and bright domains in PVA/N,P-CDs30 are obvious in the height drop range of 50 nm, and the results show that for a N,P-CDs content of 30%, the N,P-CDs will aggregate and lead to phase separation. In addition, the morphology of the films was observed using polarizing microscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. S3. No particles are observed in PVA, PVA/N,P-CDs10 and PVA/N,P-CDs20 films. However, spherical particles begin to appear in the PVA/N,P-CDs30 film, and the results show that the hydrogen bonding between N,P-CDs and PVA reaches saturation for a N,P-CDs content of 30%. The N,P-CDs are attached to the surface of the film, leading to a modification of the roughness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of PVA/N,P-CDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Good mechanical properties are extremely important for the application of film. However, in general, traditional flame retardant approaches substantially reduce the mechanical performance due to interfacial interactions and heterogeneous dispersion [30]. The tensile test results of the PVA/N,P-CDs composite films are exhibited in Fig. 4 a–c. The tensile strength of the pure PVA film was 26.18 MPa, and the elongation at break was 58.75%. For the PVA/N,P-CDs composite films, both the tensile strength and elongation at break increased with increasing N,P-CDs content. For the PVA/N,P-CDs20 film with good flame retardant properties, the tensile strength and elongation at break were 52.85 MPa and 147.26%, respectively. These values were approximately 2 times and 3 times those of the pure PVA film, respectively. The mechanical properties of the PVA/N,P-CDs composite films were considerably improved, which is attributable to the formation of the hydrogen-bonded crosslinking network structure between the N,P-CDs with rich hydrophilic groups on the surface and the side groups in PVA molecular chain (Fig. 4d), which enhanced the cohesive energy density of the material [31]. However, when the N,P-CDs content is 30%, the improvement of tensile strength and elongation at break of the composite films are not obvious, which may caused by the saturation of the hydrogen bond between N,P-CDs and PVA. Therefore, it is important to consider the content of N,P-CDs in the composite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability and fire behavior of PVA/N,P-CDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Thermal decomposition behaviours are crucial for investigating the combustion behaviour of polymeric materials [14]. The TGA and DTG curves of PVA and its composite films are presented in Fig. 5 , and the corresponding thermal data are shown in Table 1 . As shown in Fig. 5a, All films underwent two degradation stages. For the pure PVA film, most of the weight loss occurred between 220 and 400 °C. The degradation process began with the elimination of side groups, thereby resulting in the formation of large quantities of water and small flammable molecules, such as aldehydes, ketones and benzene. The remaining part of the film produced a conjugated structure with higher thermal stability and underwent further cross-linking with the cyclized structure. The second stage occurred between 420 and 500 °C and mainly involved random chain scission and cyclization [32]. In the PVA/N,P-CDs composite films, a small amount of phosphoric acid in the N,P-CDs solution and its abundant surface functional groups, such as –NH2, –OH, COOH and PVA molecular side groups, were eliminated in the first stage (180–250 °C). In the corresponding DTG curve (Fig. 5b), as the content of N,P-CDs increases, the T1 temperature of the composite films gradually decreases. This is because the increase in N,P-CDs content leads to an increased content of side groups and phosphoric acid in the composite film, indicating that the addition of the N,P-CDs solution promotes the pyrolysis of PVA. The second stage occurs between 380 and 480 °C. In this temperature range, the main reaction processes are crosslinking, chain breaking and cyclization between the PVA chains. At this stage, the thermal decomposition temperature of all composite films was the same as that of highly pure PVA, corresponding to the increase in T2 with the increase in N,P-CDs solution content (Table 1), indicating that the addition of N,P-CDs solution can effectively improve the stability of PVA, and at 600 °C, PVA/N,P-CDs composite films have a significant increase in residual carbon content (from 12.4 % pure PVA to 36.4 % PVA/N,P-CDs30). Such a high char residue protects the underlying PVA from heat flux and block degradation. Therefore, the flame retardancy of PVA is improved [33].

The flame retardant properties of pure PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs composite films were comparatively investigated by LOI and UL 94 measurements. Table 2 summarizes the relative data, and Fig. 6 exhibits the images captured from the combustion video. The pure PVA film burns violently, and the droplet phenomenon is serious during the combustion process. The droplets quickly ignite the absorbent cotton, and the pure PVA film shows almost no residue after 6 s, as shown in Fig. 7 (first line). Moreover, the LOI for the pure PVA film is only 19.4% (Table 2). Upon adding 10 wt% N,P-CDs solution, the droplet phenomenon disappears, and the LOI increases slightly to 24.1%. In particular, the PVA/N,P-CDs20 film burns slowly and produces a small flame when burned for 3 s. After 5 s, the film is quickly extinguished after it is removed from the fire source, after which the film is exposed for a second time to the fire source for 3 s. It is difficult for the residual film to ignite, and only a glinting flame was observed that extinguished quickly on the edge of the film. The PVA/N,P-CDs20 film successfully passed the UL-94 VTM-0 rating with an LOI value of 30.2%. Similarly, the PVA/N,P-CDs30 film passed the UL-94 VTM-0 rating with an LOI of 33.2%, and the flame on the film was instantly extinguished when the igniter was moved away. Thus, the addition of more than 20 wt% N,P-CDs can effectively enhance the flame retardancy of pure PVA. Moreover, it is worth mentioning that the N,P-CDs were applied after diluting 30 times. The actual amount of N,P-CDs added is very low, but they endow films with effective flame retardant properties.

MCC is a rapid method of identifying flame retardancy. Fig. 7 shows the heat-release rate (HRR) curves of pure PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs films, and Table 2 presents the primary information obtained from the HRR curves. In general, the higher the HRR is, the more heat is fed back to the surface of the material by combustion and the faster the flame spreads [29]. The pure PVA film burns violently, and the heat release rate (HRR) of the film reaches 211.1 and 306.3 w/g at 265.4 ℃ and 439.7 ℃, respectively. Finally, the total heat release (THR) of the film during the whole combustion process is 21.2 kJ/g. The HRR value of the PVA/N,P-CDs composite films decreases with an increase in the N,P-CDs content, and the THR decreases from 13.2 KJ/g to 6.8 KJ/g, which indicates that the addition of N,P-CDs can effectively delay and reduce the heat release of PVA while improving the flame retardancy because the N,P-CDs act as a carbon source and increase the residual char. A small amount of phosphoric acid present in the N,P-CDs solution acts as an acid source, which effectively promotes the dehydration carbonization and cyclization of PVA to form a thicker char layer and prevent heat transfer between the flame and the matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the residual char and flame retardant mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: In situ infrared spectroscopy was used to further analyze the flame retardant effect of N,P-CDs on PVA with respect to the gaseous material. As shown in Fig. 8 a and b, for both PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs20, no degraded gas products were detected below 200 °C. However, some similar pyrolysis products can be observed from their FTIR spectra, such as the O–H (3269 cm−1), N–H/O–H/P–H (3287 cm−1), C–H (2920 and 2936 cm−1) and C–O bonds of PVA (1428 cm−1) as well as the C–O–C bonds of other molecular species (1086 and 1083 cm−1). New absorption peaks appear at 1233 cm−1 and 999 cm−1, which are assigned to the C–N and P–O bonds of the N,P-CDs solution in the PVA/N,P-CDs20 film, respectively. When the temperature is higher than 280°C, the absorption peaks of NH3 (915 cm−1) are detected in the PVA/N,P-CDs20 film. [34,35] As an inert gas, NH3 can dilute the fuel and oxygen concentrations.

For the burnt condensed phase, the char residue morphology of the PVA/N,P-CDs30 film was investigated by SEM and EDS to identify the flame retardancy of the N,P-CDs. The SEM (Fig. 9 a and b) for the residual char after combustion shows numerous carbon particles with a diameter of 50–160 nm and small pores on the surface of the combustion interface due to dehydration of the matrix of the PVA/N,P-CDs composite film, gas release and aggregation of N,P-CDs in the combustion process. Therefore, a carbon protective layer was formed that effectively retarded the thermal decomposition of the underlying PVA matrix, which enhanced the flame retardancy. Furthermore, the EDS diagrams and element content (Fig. 9c–g) show that the char residue contains C, O, P and N at concentrations of 84.94, 11.79, 2.59 and 0.68 wt%, respectively. The lower nitrogen residue indicates that most of the nitrogen is released in the form of NH3, and the higher carbon residue further indicates that the PVA/N,P-CDs composite film forms a thicker protective char layer after burning. Meanwhile, a Raman spectrometer was applied to investigate the proportion of graphitized structures in the residual char of PVA and PVA/N,P-CDs30 films, and the results are shown in Fig. 10 a and b, respectively. The structure of the char residues can provide important information regarding flame retardant mechanisms. In general, the ratio of the integrated intensities of the D (1350 cm−1) to G (1580 cm−1) bands (ID/IG) can be used to estimate the graphitized structure in the residual char [36]. Compared with the PVA film (ID/IG = 3.54), the PVA/N,P-CDs30 film shows a lower ID/IG value (ID/IG = 2.31), which indicates that the graphitized structure of the PVA/N,P-CDs30 film is higher than that of the PVA film. This is due to the introduction of N,P-CDs into the graphite carbon core and the complex chemical reaction between the N,P-CDs solution and PVA decomposition products. Thus, the strength of the char layer is enhanced and becomes more compact and stable, leading to a better barrier effect during combustion.

According to the above research results, the flame retardant mechanism of the PVA/N,P-CDs composite film can be described as follows: The addition of N,P-CDs increases the carbon residue rate and releases ammonia during combustion. Volatilized inert gases can effectively dilute the concentration of oxygen and fuel and remove heat. In addition, a small amount of phosphoric acid in the N,P-CDs solution can effectively promote the dehydration, crosslinking and cyclization of the PVA matrix. Phosphoric acid can also form phosphoric acid derivatives in the combustion process. Volatile phosphoric acid derivatives can release phosphorus containing active free radicals to capture H · and OH · free radicals in the combustion reaction [37], and, finally, form a continuous and tight condensed carbonization structure resulting in a good flame retardant effect on the PVA matrix. According to our experimental results and relevant references [38–40], the possible flame retardant mechanism of N,P-CDs for PVA is presented in Scheme 2 .

In this study, N,P-CDs were successfully synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal strategy. The XPS and FTIR spectra indicated the presence of phosphate and amino groups on the surface of the N,P-CDs. Furthermore, a novel flame retardant PVA composite film was prepared by mixing N,P-CDs into a PVA matrix. The fireproofing tests indicated that a small concentration of N,P-CDs can enable the PVA film to reach the VTM-0 level and obtain satisfactory flame retardancy. The N,P-CDs did not affect the film transparency and effectively improved the mechanical strength and thermal stability. Most importantly, the doping of the N,P-CDs endowed the film with UV absorption and significant anti-dripping abilities. These improvements were attributable to the uniform and high char-yielding effects of the N,P-CDs, and the less phosphoric acid in the N,P-CDs solution effectively promotes the dehydration, crosslinking and cyclization of the PVA matrix. The evolution results from a series of complicated chemical reactions between the decomposition products of N,P-CDs and PVA.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the Guangxi Natural Science Project of China (No.) and the Innovation and entrepreneurship training program for Guangxi District college students in 2020.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpolymj.2021.110970.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
